---
title: "pudo-sequences"
subtitle: "Pickup/drop-off sequence combinatorics for local routing decisions."
---

`pudo-sequences` generates only the event orders that satisfy paired pickup and
drop-off precedence constraints. It is useful when a routing, dispatching,
simulation, optimization, or learning system needs a compact local action space
before evaluating distance, time windows, capacity, cost, or reward.

```text
pickup_i before dropoff_i
```

The package does not solve a route. It gives the constrained sequence space that
a solver, simulator, heuristic, or policy can evaluate.

## Install

```bash
pip install pudo-sequences
```

The core package has no runtime dependency beyond Python. The optional
topological-sort strategy uses NetworkX:

```bash
pip install "pudo-sequences[networkx]"
```

## Quick start

With the default integer convention, pickup `i` maps to drop-off `i + n`, where
`n` is the number of new requests.

```python
from pudo_sequences import count_pudo_sequences, pudo_sequences

print(count_pudo_sequences(2))
print(pudo_sequences([0, 1]))
```

```text
6
[(0, 1, 2, 3), (0, 1, 3, 2), (0, 2, 1, 3),
 (1, 0, 2, 3), (1, 0, 3, 2), (1, 3, 0, 2)]
```

For `requests=[0, 1]`, events `0` and `1` are pickups, while events `2` and
`3` are the paired drop-offs.

## Why it matters

Normal permutations include impossible orders. For two requests, there are
`24` unconstrained permutations of four events, but only `6` valid PU/DO
sequences.

| Requests | Unconstrained permutations | Valid PU/DO sequences | Valid share |
| ---: | ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 1 | 2 | 1 | 50.0% |
| 2 | 24 | 6 | 25.0% |
| 3 | 720 | 90 | 12.5% |
| 4 | 40,320 | 2,520 | 6.25% |
| 5 | 3,628,800 | 113,400 | 3.125% |

This gap is why it is usually better to generate the constrained local action
set directly instead of generating impossible actions first and filtering later.
